body:
### Problem

There is no text-object which applies to the entire buffer. To apply an operator to an entire buffer you currently need to do something like `ggVG`, which moves the cursor. This makes it inconvenient to, for example, yank or format an entire buffer.

### Expected behavior

Introduce a default text-object for the entire buffer.

[Discussed on Matrix here](https://matrix.to/#/!cylwlNXSwagQmZSkzs:matrix.org/$W3yEAapnfS2D_Da65WnRSKFJSnXp_ls7GEc3c2sjHAM?via=mozilla.org&via=matrix.org&via=gitter.im). Suggested keymaps that were proposed where `al` (for all lines) and `<CR>` (just kinda feels right imo).

comment_data.body:
I suggest e.

https://github.com/kana/vim-textobj-entire

has been around for a while.

I haven't worked with textobjects - would be down to write a draft for this.

I vote against `e` because that's already a useful motion (for example `gUe` to capitalize the rest of the word). I dont necessarily think `<CR>` is ideal, but that is at least somewhat superfluous with `j`.

Here's the snippet I posted on matrix you can use as a jumping off point if you want. It restores cursor position and doesn't pollute the jumplist:

```lua
vim.keymap.set("o", "<CR>", function()
  local pos = vim.api.nvim_win_get_cursor(0)
  vim.cmd("keepj norm! gg" .. vim.v.operator .. "G")
  vim.api.nvim_win_set_cursor(0, pos)
end)
```
I use `g` cause its already related to `ggVG` or actually more precisely - `ag` or `ig`

`il` for "In current Line" and `al` for "All Lines".

`e` is a poor mnemonic, especially in the form of `ie` and `ae`. whereas "In Line" is a useful concept that currently lacks a textobject, and "All Lines" is only useful when it includes EOL.

I wish matchit didn't already use `%`, because it would have been the obvious pick otherwise (imo). Oh well.

> I vote against `e` because that's already a useful motion

They meant `ae`. See the plugin they linked.

I use `ag` since it's easy to remember, thanks to `gg` and `G`. It also keeps `l` free for something else, which is useful since many words start with `l`: "last", "line", "list", "link", "loop", "local", "log", "lambda", "label", "literal" ...

There are only two issues with `ag`:

1. I need to remap `gqga` to `gqag` because I sometimes mistype this combination.
2. I have no idea what `ig` can be used for. It could just be the same as `ag` for now.

But `al` and `il` are also great choices. At least not `ae`.

I like the idea for l and have no attachment to e personally

However, il being current and al being all lines doesn't sit right right me. Namely the "a" implies around, not all. cap doesn't mean changes around all paragraphs, now caw with all words.

with justin's example, the same l would refer to different text objects. of course it'd be more useful but doesnt quite strike me as correct

> with justin's example, the same l would refer to different text objects. of course it'd be more useful but doesnt quite strike me as correct

that's true, but all that really matters is that things are mnemonic and practical. that's the main motivation for an "entire buffer" textobject, after all.

`ag` would be my vote, but all of the presented options are very good. I suppose we're just waiting now to see if vim wants this before we do anything else here.

As for what `ig`/`il`/`ie` should do, we should consider copying from vim-textobj-entire.

I humbly suggest `ad`  for document or `af` for file: mnemonic and avoids the inconsistency of "all lines" instead of "around lines".
Whether there can be a useful distinction between `id` and `ad`, such that the former deserves to be implemented, would be up for debate.

I use `i_` and `a_`

- While not a mnemonic per se, it is in line with `_` and `g_`
- `a_` is a big selection that I don't want to be making by accident
- I don't use `i_` that much, so I don't want it taking up more commonly used real estate.

> I humbly suggest `ad` for document or `af` for file: mnemonic and avoids the inconsistency of "all lines" instead of "around lines". Whether there can be a useful distinction between `id` and `ad`, such that the former deserves to be implemented, would be up for debate.

Doesn't matter *that* much, but `ad` and `af` are what treesitter-textobjects uses in its documentation for conditional and function call. The function call object is quite useful.

> This makes it inconvenient to, for example, yank or format an entire buffer.

I mean we do have ways to select the entire buffer. We can use % to select the entire buffer in command so it would be `:%y` to yank the entire buffer. I think having a convenient text motion makes more sense.


> `il` for "In current Line" and `al` for "All Lines".

I think this would change the typical way we "go" after an operator from line to line.

Currently we see the motion for grabbing  lines to be `g|G` oriented -> `Operator|g|g` being for example `cgg` is changing from the current line up and `Operator|G` being current line down. Should we perhaps consider the `gG` movement to be all lines ie from g(first line) to G(last line)?

However, `ae` and `ie` conflict with "environment", which is widely used in TeX (e.g., vimtex uses it). My solution is to use `A` instead. For example, the following Vim mappings use `A` to select the entire buffer in visual-block mode (with `ggVG`), automatically (un)mapping it when entering/exiting visual-block mode (Ctrl-V, represented as `\x16`):

```vim
" visual-block
augroup visual-block | au!
    autocmd ModeChanged [\x16]:* xmap A :<C-U>normal! ggVG<CR>
    autocmd ModeChanged *:[\x16] xunmap A
augroup end
omap A <Cmd>normal! ggVG<CR>
xmap A :<C-U>normal! ggVG<CR>
```

For reference: https://codeberg.org/mao-yining/dotfile_vim/src/commit/792ee24d243b26f6c5eabf035fa205f2b16b2afa/plugin/mappings.vim#L189-L212

It's been a month with no interaction on the associated Vim issue. I think it probably makes sense to work on this now (are you still interested, @barrettruth?) and if Vim ever wants this they could downstream it.

As for deciding what mapping to use, I'm a fan of `ag`, although I don't have a particularly strong opinion among the options presented here. I say we just pick one and go for it.

Please ues justin's `il` and `al` if so as both `in` and `around` mapping make sense. Just a vote. You can hide/delete it, if it's add to noice.

 Btw `g*` text object pretty popular to have miscellaneous  like `gh` for diff text-object, `gc` for comment text-object.

## Survey. Vote with react 

The discussion around `il` and `al` seems more important in this case, and it should probably be resolved before adding an entire-buffer textobject. Ideally, this should be discussed in a separate issue.

#### 1. `il` selects the current line, while `al` selects the whole buffer

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Solves the problem without adding extra textobjects | Introduces ambiguity, making it an exception |

If you prefer this option, react with ❤️

#### 2. `il` and `al` work only on the current line, `ag` (or similar) for the entire buffer

- `il` selects the line contents without leading/trailing whitespace (similar to `^vg_`)
- `al` selects the whole line (similar to `V`)

This matches the existing `ip` / `ap` mental model. A separate mapping such as `ag` could then be used for selecting the entire buffer.

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Avoids semantic ambiguity | The additional textobjects may have questionable practical value |
| Easy to understand and remember | They are simple enough to implement manually |
| More flexible overall (`il`, `al`, `ig`, `ag`) | |

If you prefer this option, react with 🚀

#### 3. No line textobject, only `ag` (or similar) for the entire buffer

Vim already provides many ways to work with the current line without a dedicated textobject, for example:

`dd` `cc` `V` `v$` `vg_` `gcc` `gww` `gqq` ...

Adding a line textobject might discourage conventional workflows, even though I personally think it would still be an improvement.

If you prefer this option, react with 👀

> Vim already provides many ways to work with the current line without a dedicated textobject,

Those include the EOL, whereas `il` would be characterwise (no EOL). And conversely, a "whole buffer without EOL" textobject is not useful. This is why it makes sense to use `l` for this dual purpose.

I think it also depends on whether there is a practical use case for selecting a line without leading or trailing spaces with `il` (and `al` for the whole line).

In that case, something like `ag` without `ig` could be used (still a strange exception). For reference, the `ag` textobject was taken from [doc/mini-ai.txt](https://github.com/nvim-mini/mini.ai/blob/main/doc/mini-ai.txt#L497-L506).

The ideas that came to mind right away for an `il` textobject are:

1. Wrapping a line in a function call. In practice, though, I would prefer a function that handles spacing automatically even if the whole line is selected.
2. `vilr<char>` or some unusual character-rotation workflows.

But these examples are fairly bad. Is there anything useful?

> whether there is a practical use case for selecting a line without leading or trailing spaces with `il`

oh I forgot about that. I think it's useful, I often want it. For example in a `:terminal` buffer where there is lots of useless "whitespace" because the job colored a background or whatever.

We've discussed this long enough, next step is to just do it. If someone has a strong objection later we can revisit.
working on it now (commenting to prevent duplication of efforts)

After cloning #39845 to my machine to test it, I have to say I really like both of these new text-objects. However, as [has been pointed out](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39209#issuecomment-4276520963), the text-objects `al` and `il` don't really match; one operates on the inside of a single line, while one refers to an entire buffer's line.

I think it might make since to have two different pairs of text objects: `al`/`il` to act on lines, and `a?`/`i?` to act on the whole buffer (`?` being the stand-in for the letter to use, such as `e` or `g`, and not a literal `?`). This would give us four text-objects:
- `a?`: Text object for all lines in the buffer. Renamed from `al`. (same as `ae` in [vim-textobj-entire](https://github.com/kana/vim-textobj-entire))
- `i?`: All lines in the buffer, excluding leading and trailing empty lines. (same as `ie` in [vim-textobj-entire](https://github.com/kana/vim-textobj-entire))
- `al`: Changed from "all lines" to "the current line, including leading and trailing whitespace, but not including EOL". So while `dd` deletes a line, `dal` would delete the *contents* of a line.
- `il`: Remains the same as the current `il`, being the current line, excluding leading and trailing whitespace.

This makes `al` and `il` be much more of a pair, instead of being rather unrelated. I do also believe that a version of `il` that includes whitespace would be a useful addition along with `il`.

I admit `i?` would be a rather niche text-object, but it has precedent ([vim-textobj-entire](https://github.com/kana/vim-textobj-entire)), and is a much more intuitive fit for `a?`'s paired text-object.

---

Unless we're all in unanimous agreement with my suggestion, I say we still get #39845 merged as is (once it's ready, ofc), and we can make these changes in a separate PR.
